markdown:
Based on the reported model success percentages from each paper, here is the ranking from **easiest to hardest** (higher model success = easier benchmark):

## Ranking: Easiest → Hardest

**1. GAIA (easiest)** — Best reported model success: ≤30% (GPT-4 with plugins, on Level 1 only; 0% on Level 3). [1]

**2. WebArena** — Best reported model success: 14.41% (GPT-4 with CoT prompting + UA hint); 11.70% for GPT-4 with CoT only. [2] [3]

**3. SWE-bench (hardest)** — Best reported model success: 1.96% (Claude 2, BM25 retrieval at 13k context); 4.8% with "oracle" retrieval. [4] [5]

## Summary Table

| Rank | Benchmark | Best Model | Reported Success % |
|------|-----------|------------|--------------------|
| 1 (easiest) | GAIA | GPT-4 + plugins (Level 1) | ≤30% |
| 2 | WebArena | GPT-4 + CoT + UA hint | 14.41% |
| 3 (hardest) | SWE-bench | Claude 2 | 1.96% |

The ranking is driven entirely by the cited success-rate figures: GAIA's best headline number is the highest, WebArena's best is mid-range, and SWE-bench's best is by far the lowest — so SWE-bench is the hardest of the three for current models.

Sources
[1] GAIA:, Level 3, p. 3
[2] WE BAR E N A: A REALISTIC WEB ENVIRONMENT FOR BUILDING AUTONOMOUS AGENTS, 5 RESULTS, p. 8
[3] WE BAR E N A: A REALISTIC WEB ENVIRONMENT FOR BUILDING AUTONOMOUS AGENTS, 5 RESULTS, p. 8
[4] SWE-BENCH: CAN LANGUAGE MODELS RESOLVE REAL-WORLD GITHUB ISSUES?, ABSTRACT, p. 1
[5] SWE-BENCH: CAN LANGUAGE MODELS RESOLVE REAL-WORLD GITHUB ISSUES?, 4.3 MODELS, p. 6
